stage.name: Verification questions: turn architecture into checks
stage.description: A handoff lesson for turning technical understanding into verification-ready questions.
stage.courseTemplateName: Professional Technical Deep Dive - Evidence-Grounded SlideKit

scenes.title: A good architecture reader asks verifiable questions

content.title: A good architecture reader asks verifiable questions
content.subtitle: The handoff from learning to engineering is the ability to ask what should be observed, stimulated, and checked.
content.thesis: Verification questions are architecture claims turned into checkable form.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 requirements: scope and verification intent
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The requirements document defines the CVA6 feature scope and ties requirements to verification intent and interface requirements.

content.speakerNotes: This lesson is Verification questions: turn architecture into checks. The handoff from learning to engineering is the ability to ask what should be observed, stimulated, and checked. The point is not to memorize a wall of CVA6 facts. The point is to build a disciplined source-reading habit: name the boundary, name the source, and avoid claims that the public material does not support.

actions.text: This lesson is Verification questions: turn architecture into checks. The handoff from learning to engineering is the ability to ask what should be observed, stimulated, and checked. The point is not to memorize a wall of CVA6 facts. The point is to build a disciplined source-reading habit: name the boundary, name the source, and avoid claims that the public material does not support.

scenes.title: Turn each claim into a checkable question

content.title: A claim is not useful until it can be challenged
content.subtitle: Verification begins by asking how we would know the claim is true.

cards.title: Stimulus
cards.body: What input sequence or state exposes the behavior?

cards.title: Observation
cards.body: Which output, trace, CSR, or interface signal reveals it?

cards.title: Coverage
cards.body: Which cases would convince us we did not test only the happy path?

content.takeaway: Architecture reading should create testable questions.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 requirements: scope and verification intent
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The requirements document defines the CVA6 feature scope and ties requirements to verification intent and interface requirements.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: Scoreboard submodule
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The scoreboard tracks issued instructions, stores returned results by transaction ID, and exposes the oldest completed entry to commit.

content.speakerNotes: Verification handoff is where learning becomes useful. If we say the scoreboard tracks returned results by transaction ID, how would we observe that? What stimulus would create out-of-order result return? What invariant must hold at commit? What coverage tells us we tested more than the easy case? These questions are the practical output of architecture reading.

actions.text: Verification handoff is where learning becomes useful. If we say the scoreboard tracks returned results by transaction ID, how would we observe that? What stimulus would create out-of-order result return? What invariant must hold at commit? What coverage tells us we tested more than the easy case? These questions are the practical output of architecture reading.

content.title: The testharness reminds us to observe boundaries
content.body: AXI requests, interrupt inputs, debug paths, RVFI/tracer output, and peripheral responses can become observation points for verification thinking.
content.caption: Boundary diagrams help turn architecture into observable checks.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 APU: testharness
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The testharness connects CVA6, debug, CLINT, PLIC, timer, ROM, UART, DRAM, and other slaves through AXI-related structures.

content.speakerNotes: The testharness chapter is not a complete verification plan, but it reminds us of observation points. AXI requests and responses, interrupt sources, debug paths, and RVFI or tracer outputs all provide ways to observe behavior. A good course should train learners to ask: where could I see this claim become true or false?

actions.text: The testharness chapter is not a complete verification plan, but it reminds us of observation points. AXI requests and responses, interrupt sources, debug paths, and RVFI or tracer outputs all provide ways to observe behavior. A good course should train learners to ask: where could I see this claim become true or false?

scenes.title: A verification handoff should be small and useful

content.title: Write questions the verification owner can actually use

items.title: Stimulus
items.body: The scenario that should expose it.

items.title: Observation
items.body: The signal, trace, CSR, or output to inspect.

content.takeaway: Useful questions are short, scoped, and evidence-backed.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 requirements: scope and verification intent
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The requirements document defines the CVA6 feature scope and ties requirements to verification intent and interface requirements.

content.speakerNotes: The handoff should not be a giant essay. One behavior, one stimulus idea, one observation point, and one corner case are enough to start a real conversation. This also makes OpenHW Explorer stronger: each lesson can end with a verification handoff that invites practical engagement.

actions.text: The handoff should not be a giant essay. One behavior, one stimulus idea, one observation point, and one corner case are enough to start a real conversation. This also makes OpenHW Explorer stronger: each lesson can end with a verification handoff that invites practical engagement.

scenes.title: Check understanding: Verification questions: turn architecture into checks

questions.question: What makes an architecture claim useful for verification?

options.label: It can be turned into stimulus, observation, invariant, and coverage questions

questions.question: Which are valid observation points in a CVA6 verification handoff?
questions.analysis: Visual styling is not a verification observation point.

questions.question: What should a small handoff include?

options.label: Behavior, stimulus, observation, and corner case

questions.analysis: This format is short enough to be used.

questions.question: Why are non-happy-path cases important?

options.label: They reveal whether the architecture understanding survives stalls, exceptions, and boundaries
They make all tests slower for no reason

questions.analysis: Corner cases often reveal the real architecture contract.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: COMMIT_STAGE module
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: COMMIT_STAGE updates architectural state, writes CSR/register state, commits stores, and handles precise traps in order.

content.speakerNotes: This quiz checks the core judgment of Verification questions: turn architecture into checks. The right answers should feel less like trivia and more like disciplined classification: what is the boundary, what is the source, what is the limitation, and what would we check next?

scenes.title: Summary: Verification questions: turn architecture into checks

content.title: Verification questions: turn architecture into checks
takeaways: Write better verification handoff questions after reading CVA6 source and documentation.
Turn every public explanation into an evidence-backed learning artifact.
content.nextStep: Next: final project brief, where the learner gets a concrete capstone task.
content.closing: This is how OpenHW Explorer can teach serious open hardware without drifting into unsupported certainty.

content.speakerNotes: To summarize: Write better verification handoff questions after reading CVA6 source and documentation. Keep the claim small, keep the source visible, and keep the boundary honest. Next: final project brief, where the learner gets a concrete capstone task.
